q: Which DPA produces mydriasis WITHOUT cycloplegia?
w: Phenylephrine is an α1 agonist on the dilator; the ciliary muscle has no α1, so accommodation is untouched.

q: Why does phenylephrine spare accommodation?
o: α1 agonist; ciliary muscle has no α1
It relaxes the ciliary muscle directly
It blocks M3 only on the sphincter
w: Phenylephrine acts on sympathetic α1 (dilator); the ciliary muscle is purely M3, so it is unaffected.
k: α1 dilator only; ciliary muscle is M3.

q: The preferred dilating agent in pregnancy is:
w: All DPAs are FDA Category C; tropicamide has the shortest duration and least systemic exposure.

q: Pressing the inner canthus for 2 minutes after instillation (nasolacrimal occlusion) serves to:
o: Stop the drug working on the eye
w: NLO blocks drainage through the nasolacrimal duct, limiting systemic side effects.

q: Which DPA should NEVER be prescribed for home use, because it masks corneal damage?
w: Proparacaine abolishes corneal sensation; chronic use lets injury progress unfelt (neurotrophic keratopathy).

q: Fluorescein must NOT be used when the patient is wearing:
w: Fluorescein permanently stains hydrogel (soft) lenses; remove them first.

q: All mydriatic DPAs share which absolute contraindication?
w: Dilation can push the iris into the angle and trigger acute angle-closure.

q: The strongest cycloplegic among the routinely used DPAs for pediatric refraction is:
w: Cyclopentolate gives stronger, more reliable cycloplegia than tropicamide (atropine is stronger but not a routine DPA).

q: Tropicamide and cyclopentolate dilate the pupil by:
o: Stimulating α1 on the dilator muscle
Blocking M3 so the sphincter relaxes
w: As M3 antagonists they relax the sphincter; the unopposed dilator opens the pupil (and the ciliary muscle relaxes = cycloplegia).
k: M3 block = sphincter relaxes, dilator opens.

q: Proparacaine works by:
w: Proparacaine is a sodium-channel blocking ester anesthetic with rapid onset and short duration.

q: Fluorescein fluoresces best under which light?
w: Fluorescein absorbs blue near 465 nm and emits green, so defects glow under cobalt blue.

q: How many DPAs are approved under RA 8050?
w: Five: phenylephrine, tropicamide, cyclopentolate, proparacaine, fluorescein (plus authorized miotics).

q: Cyclopentolate at 2% in a young child risks which adverse effect?
w: A child's immature liver lets the drug accumulate and cross into the CNS; use 0.5–1% and apply NLO.

q: By what age should an infant's eyes be consistently straight?
w: Stable ocular alignment is expected by 3–4 months; persistent deviation beyond this warrants evaluation.

q: Which visual acuity test is used for non-verbal infants?
w: Preferential looking (Teller cards) measures infant VA by gaze preference for a grating.

q: For a cooperative 3–5 year old, an appropriate VA test is:
w: Matching/picture tests (HOTV, Lea Symbols, Allen) suit preschoolers who cannot read letters.

q: Which amblyopia type has the WORST prognosis?
w: Deprivation amblyopia (cataract, ptosis) has the worst prognosis and must be treated within the critical period.

q: The first treatment step for amblyopia is:
w: Full spectacle correction comes first; patching is added only if VA does not normalize with glasses alone.

q: Pseudostrabismus (from wide epicanthal folds) is identified by:
w: Symmetric corneal reflexes confirm no true deviation; it resolves as the nasal bridge develops, no treatment needed.

q: The first management of accommodative esotropia is:
w: Full plus removes the accommodative drive; surgery is reserved for residual deviation after maximal correction.

q: The critical period for amblyopia treatment peaks and closes at about:
w: Sensitivity peaks 1–3 years and largely closes by 7–8, with limited gains possible to ~12.

q: A 4-year-old has 20/80 OS, 20/20 OD, and a 3.50 D hyperopic difference. The diagnosis and first step:
w: A large refractive difference with reduced VA in the more ametropic eye is anisometropic amblyopia; correct first, then add patching if needed.

q: Patching in amblyopia is added:
o: Always as the very first step
w: Glasses are trialed for 12–16 weeks; patching is added if VA does not normalize with correction alone.
k: Patch only if glasses don't normalize VA.

q: Amblyopia improvement is still possible up to about what age (less effectively)?
w: Treatment is less effective after 7 but improvement is still possible up to ~12; minimal gains thereafter.

q: Which condition needs NO treatment, only reassurance?
w: Pseudostrabismus resolves with facial growth; the others all require active management.

q: The frequency of dry vs wet age-related macular degeneration is:
w: Dry (atrophic) is ~85%, but wet (~15%) causes the majority of SEVERE vision loss.

q: Which type of AMD causes the MAJORITY of severe vision loss?
w: Although less common, wet AMD with choroidal neovascularization causes most severe central vision loss.
k: Wet AMD = most severe vision loss.

q: AREDS2 supplements:
w: AREDS2 reduces progression risk by about 25% in intermediate AMD; it does not treat or reverse disease.

q: In hypertensive retinopathy, AV nicking corresponds to which Keith-Wagener-Barker grade?
w: AV nicking (and silver wiring) is grade 2; grade 4 adds papilledema (hypertensive emergency).

q: Grade 4 hypertensive retinopathy is defined by:
w: Papilledema marks grade 4 = malignant/accelerated hypertension, an emergency.

q: The most common cause of vision loss in diabetic retinopathy is:
w: Diabetic macular edema (which can occur at any DR stage) is the most common cause of vision loss.
k: DR vision loss = diabetic macular edema.

q: Wet AMD is treated with:
w: Anti-VEGF agents (bevacizumab, ranibizumab, aflibercept) are first-line for neovascular AMD.

q: The drusen type carrying the highest risk of progression to advanced AMD is:
w: Soft confluent drusen carry higher conversion risk than small hard drusen.

q: Clinically significant macular edema (CSME) is defined by retinal thickening within:
w: CSME includes thickening or hard exudates within 500 µm of the foveal center; refer for anti-VEGF.

q: Sudden metamorphopsia with subretinal fluid and hemorrhage at the macula in an elderly patient is:
w: Acute distortion plus subretinal fluid/blood is wet AMD until proven otherwise; refer urgently for anti-VEGF.
k: Sudden metamorphopsia + subretinal blood = wet AMD.

q: The normal retinal artery-to-vein (A:V) caliber ratio is:
w: Normal A:V is about 2:3 (arteries narrower than veins); narrowing occurs in hypertension.

q: Severe non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy is identified by the:
w: 4-2-1: hemorrhages in 4 quadrants, OR venous beading in 2, OR IRMA in 1.

q: A pupil-involving CN3 palsy is a neurosurgical emergency because it suggests:
w: Pupillomotor fibers run on the outside of CN3, so compression (PCA aneurysm) blows the pupil early.

q: CN6 (abducens) palsy is called a 'false localizing sign' because:
o: It only ever occurs in the pons
It is congenital in all cases
Raised ICP compresses its long course
w: Its long intracranial course makes CN6 vulnerable to raised ICP anywhere, so it poorly localizes a lesion.

q: Infantile (congenital) nystagmus syndrome is characteristically:
w: INS is horizontal in all gaze and lessens with convergence (so patients read up close); vertical nystagmus is always acquired.

q: Vertical nystagmus is:
w: Vertical nystagmus is always acquired and warrants neurological workup; INS is horizontal.

q: Latent nystagmus is best handled during monocular VA testing by:
w: Occlusion triggers latent nystagmus and worsens VA; fog the eye with +5.00 to keep both eyes open.

q: Cortical visual impairment (CVI) is most commonly caused by:
w: CVI results from brain injury (perinatal hypoxia-ischemia, periventricular leukomalacia); the eyes are structurally normal.

q: In CVI, the eye examination shows:
w: CVI vision loss is cortical; the anterior and posterior segments and pupils are normal.

q: A CN4 (superior oblique) palsy causes vertical diplopia that is worst:
w: SO depresses the adducted eye, so its palsy causes vertical diplopia worst on downgaze and reading.
k: CN4 palsy = vertical diplopia worse on downgaze.

q: The head posture adopted in a CN4 palsy is a tilt:
w: Patients tilt the head away from the affected side (and chin down) to reduce the vertical deviation.
k: CN4: head tilt away, chin down.

q: The 3-step test used to localize a cyclovertical muscle palsy is the:
w: The Parks-Bielschowsky 3-step localizes the paretic cyclovertical muscle (commonly CN4/SO).

q: Jerk nystagmus is named according to the direction of its:
w: Jerk nystagmus is named for the fast phase direction; this is not how cranial nerve palsies are named.
k: Jerk nystagmus named for the fast phase.

q: A 2-year-old with periventricular leukomalacia on MRI, normal-looking eyes, and poor visual responses most likely has:
w: Neurological insult + behavioral visual difficulty + normal ocular structures = CVI.
k: PVL + normal eyes + poor vision = CVI.

q: A chemical solvent splash into the eye is first managed by:
o: Instilling proparacaine and referring without irrigation
Immediate irrigation with water/saline for 20–30 minutes
w: Irrigation is the first and most critical action regardless of the chemical; history follows.

q: The CVS (computer vision syndrome) '20-20-20 rule' means:
w: Every 20 minutes, look ~20 feet away for 20 seconds to relax accommodation.

q: Under RA 11210 (2019), maternity leave in the Philippines is:
w: The Expanded Maternity Leave Law (RA 11210) provides 105 days.

q: Paternity leave under RA 8187 is:
w: RA 8187 grants 7 working days of paternity leave.

q: The best lens material for protective (safety) eyewear is:
w: Polycarbonate is the most impact-resistant material, the choice for safety eyewear.

q: Welding without protection classically causes:
w: UV exposure in arc welding causes photokeratitis; infrared exposure (glassblowing) causes lens cataract.

q: Infrared (IR) occupational exposure (e.g., glassblowing) classically causes:
w: IR causes thermal damage and the glassblower's cataract (posterior subcapsular).

q: The Occupational Safety and Health Standards (OSHS) in the Philippines fall under which agency?
w: OSHS is administered by the Department of Labor and Employment (DOLE).

q: For a chemical splash hazard, a face shield should be used:
o: In addition to sealed splash goggles
w: A face shield protects the face but does not seal the eyes; it is used WITH splash goggles, not instead.

q: Which is NOT appropriate management for computer vision syndrome?
w: Cycloplegics are diagnostic agents; daily home use abolishes accommodation and is never appropriate.

q: GSIS covers which workers, and SSS which?
w: GSIS covers government employees; SSS covers the private sector.

q: A professional driver's occupational eye exam must specifically include:
w: Drivers need adequate visual fields and color vision (signals); exams are tailored to job demands.

q: Which tint best improves visibility in fog, haze, and low light?
w: Yellow tints enhance contrast in haze and low light by cutting blue scatter.

q: Which tint best enhances depth perception for golf or tennis?
w: Brown/amber tints contain a red element that enhances contrast and depth judgment.

q: A gray tint is best described as:
w: Gray reduces overall brightness without changing color balance or improving contrast.

q: Difficulty tracking a fast-moving ball despite 20/20 static VA indicates a deficit in:
w: Dynamic VA (moving targets) is independent of static VA and is tested with a tachistoscope or Marsden ball.

q: Which instrument assesses dynamic visual acuity?
w: The tachistoscope (brief target presentation) and the Marsden ball (3D moving target) assess dynamic VA.

q: The Wayne Saccadic Fixator is used to train:
w: The Wayne Saccadic Fixator trains eye-hand coordination, peripheral awareness, and reaction speed.

q: Contrast sensitivity in sports vision is measured with the:
w: The Pelli-Robson chart measures contrast sensitivity, important for low-contrast sport situations.

q: The ASTM F803 protective eyewear standard applies specifically to:
w: ASTM F803 governs racquet-sport eye protectors; other sports use their own standards.

q: Do contact lenses provide impact or UV protection in sports?
w: Contact lenses offer no impact protection, so protective eyewear must still be worn.
k: CLs give no protection; still need eyewear.

q: A static VA of 20/20 in an athlete does NOT guarantee good:
w: Dynamic VA, depth perception, eye-hand coordination, and peripheral awareness are independent of static VA.

q: The best protective eyewear lens material for athletes is:
w: Polycarbonate's impact resistance makes it the standard for sports eye protection.

q: The Marsden ball trains and tests primarily:
w: A suspended moving ball trains pursuit tracking and dynamic visual acuity.

q: The WHO definition of LOW VISION is best-corrected acuity in the better eye worse than:
w: Low vision = worse than 20/70; blindness = 20/200 or worse in the better eye.

q: The WHO definition of BLINDNESS is best-corrected acuity in the better eye of:
w: Blindness is defined as 20/200 or worse in the better eye.

q: The reference standard relating magnification to add power is:
w: The reference is 4 D = 1×, so equivalent add = M × 4.

q: Required magnification is calculated as:
w: M = required VA / present VA (e.g., N32 → N8 = 32/8 = 4×).

q: A patient reads N32 and needs N8. The magnification and equivalent add are:
w: M = 32/8 = 4×; equivalent add = 4 × 4 = +16 D (working distance 100/16 ≈ 6.25 cm).

q: Magnification should be calculated from:
w: Always refract first; magnification is based on best-corrected acuity, not uncorrected.

q: The goal of low vision rehabilitation is:
w: Low vision care maximizes functional use of remaining vision with optical and non-optical aids; 20/20 is not the goal.

q: Eccentric viewing training is appropriate for:
w: Eccentric viewing helps any patient with a central scotoma use a healthier retinal area.

q: For a patient with hand tremor needing sustained reading, the best magnifier is a:
w: A stand magnifier rests on the page at a fixed focal distance, avoiding the instability of a hand-held device.

q: Telescopes in low vision are primarily for:
w: Telescopes magnify for distance; most low vision patients need near magnification first.

q: The most common cause of blindness worldwide is:
w: Cataract is the leading cause of blindness globally, and over 80% of blindness is avoidable.

q: The equivalent add for a required 5× magnification is:
w: Equivalent add = M × 4 = 5 × 4 = +20 D (working distance 5 cm).

q: Approximately what fraction of global blindness is avoidable (preventable or treatable)?
w: About 80% of blindness is avoidable, led by cataract and uncorrected refractive error.

q: A scleral shell is used:
o: Over an existing but shrunken globe
w: A scleral shell fits over a present but shrunken eye; after enucleation the globe is gone and a custom prosthesis sits over the orbital implant.
k: Scleral shell = over a shrunken globe.

q: Immediately after eye-removal surgery, a clear placeholder that prevents socket contraction is a:
w: A conformer is a temporary clear device maintaining socket shape; the custom PMMA prosthesis is fitted at 6–8 weeks.

q: The standard of care for a definitive ocular prosthesis is:
o: A stock shell off the shelf
w: A custom PMMA prosthesis fitted by a certified ocularist gives the best fit and cosmesis; stock shells cause chronic irritation.

q: An ocular prosthesis should be professionally polished about every:
w: Polishing every 6–12 months removes deposits and surface roughness; replace the prosthesis every 5–7 years.

q: An ocular prosthesis is typically replaced every:
w: Adults replace prostheses about every 5–7 years (children more often as the orbit grows).

q: The CPF 527 (orange) absorptive filter is primarily indicated for:
w: CPF 527 cuts below 527 nm to enhance contrast and reduce glare in RP, macular disease, and glaucoma.

q: For an achromatopsia (rod monochromat) patient with severe photophobia, the best filter is:
w: A deep red/orange longpass filter cuts the short/mid wavelengths that overwhelm rods, easing photophobia.

q: Recommended eyewear for an albinism patient is:
w: Albinos lack pigment and are very light-sensitive; deep absorptive tints with full UV 400 protection are recommended.

q: Why do photochromic lenses fail to darken inside a car?
o: The cabin light is too dim
They react only to blue light
w: Photochromics need UV to activate; windshields block UV, so they stay clear while driving.
k: Windshield blocks UV → no darkening in cars.

q: An ocular prosthesis should be soaked in:
w: Tap water carries Acanthamoeba risk; use sterile saline for soaking and cleaning.
k: Soak prosthesis in sterile saline, not tap water.

q: A patient 6 months post-enucleation wears a stock prosthesis with chronic discharge and irritation. Next step:
w: Chronic irritation from a poorly fitting stock shell is corrected by a custom PMMA prosthesis; healing is complete by 6 months.
k: Stock shell irritation → custom PMMA prosthesis.

q: The custom PMMA prosthesis after eye removal is typically fitted at:
w: A conformer holds the socket initially; the custom prosthesis is fitted at ~6–8 weeks once healing is complete.

q: As originally listed in the text of RA 8050, the number of diagnostic pharmaceutical agents is:
w: RA 8050 as written lists five: phenylephrine 2.5%, tropicamide 0.5%, proparacaine 0.5%, oxybuprocaine 0.4% and fluorescein strips. Watch the wording: the 2008 IRR added cyclopentolate, so the CURRENT list is six. Five is right only when the question quotes the original statute.
k: RA 8050 original text = 5 DPAs; current list = 6 (cyclopentolate added 2008).

q: Nasolacrimal occlusion should be maintained for:
w: Two minutes of punctal pressure cuts systemic absorption through the nasal mucosa and keeps the drug on the eye, which matters most in children, pregnancy and cardiac patients.

q: The absolute contraindication to any mydriatic is:
w: Dilating a narrow angle can trigger acute angle-closure. Van Herick or gonioscopy comes before the drop.

q: Which agent must never be dispensed for home use?
w: Repeated topical anesthetic abolishes the protective blink and corneal sensation, causing epithelial breakdown and a neurotrophic ulcer. It is used in clinic only.

q: Fluorescein must not be instilled in a patient wearing:
w: Fluorescein is absorbed by hydrogel and permanently stains it. Lenses come out first, and go back in only after about an hour.
k: No fluorescein with soft lenses in.

q: An infant's eyes should be consistently straight by:
w: Intermittent misalignment is normal in the newborn, but constant deviation persisting beyond 3–4 months needs referral.

q: The appropriate acuity test for a pre-verbal infant is:
w: Preferential looking with Teller acuity cards uses an infant's innate preference for pattern over blank. HOTV and Lea suit cooperative 3–5 year olds.
k: Infant VA = preferential looking / Teller cards.

q: Which type of amblyopia carries the WORST visual prognosis?
w: Deprivation amblyopia from a dense congenital cataract or complete ptosis blocks all form vision during the critical period, so it is the densest and least reversible.

q: On Hirschberg testing, pseudostrabismus shows:
w: Pseudostrabismus, usually from prominent epicanthal folds or a wide nasal bridge, looks like esotropia but the corneal reflexes are symmetric, proving there is no true deviation.

q: The approximate split between dry and wet age-related macular degeneration is:
w: About 85% is dry and 15% wet, though the wet minority causes most of the severe central vision loss.

q: AREDS2 supplementation reduces the risk of progression to advanced AMD by about:
w: AREDS2 cuts progression risk roughly 25% in intermediate dry AMD. AREDS2 dropped beta-carotene because it raised lung cancer risk in smokers.

q: The normal retinal arteriole-to-venule ratio is:
w: Normal AV ratio is 2:3. Arteriolar narrowing in hypertensive retinopathy drops it toward 1:2 or 1:3.

q: The commonest cause of vision loss in diabetic retinopathy is:
w: Macular edema is the leading cause of diabetic visual loss and can occur at any retinopathy stage, including mild NPDR.

q: Which drusen type carries the highest risk of progression?
w: Soft, large and confluent drusen mark high risk of progression to advanced AMD. Small hard drusen are common with age and low risk.

q: CN6 palsy is a false localizing sign because raised intracranial pressure compresses the nerve along the:
w: CN6 has a long course over the petrous ridge and clivus, so raised ICP from any cause stretches it. The palsy therefore does not localize the lesion.
k: CN6 false localizing = long clival course.

q: The commonest cause of cortical visual impairment in children is:
w: CVI follows perinatal hypoxic-ischemic injury. The eyes are structurally normal, so the diagnosis rests on inconsistent visual behavior rather than fundus findings.
k: CVI = perinatal hypoxia; eyes look normal.

q: In cortical visual impairment the ocular examination shows:
w: CVI is a brain problem, so the globes look normal and pupils react. Vision fluctuates with fatigue and complexity, which distinguishes it from ocular disease.

q: Irrigation of a chemical burn should continue for:
w: Irrigate 20–30 minutes and recheck pH until neutral. Alkali keeps penetrating until diluted, so time matters more than the fluid used.
k: Chemical burn: irrigate 20–30 min to neutral pH.

q: Maternity leave under RA 11210 is:
w: RA 11210, the Expanded Maternity Leave Law, grants 105 days. RA 8187 gives 7 working days of paternity leave.

q: The Occupational Safety and Health Standards fall under which department?
w: OSHS sits with the Department of Labor and Employment, which sets workplace eye protection requirements.

q: The best tint for improving visibility in fog or haze is:
w: Yellow filters short-wavelength scatter and lifts contrast in fog, haze and flat light. Brown or amber suits depth judgment in golf and tennis.
k: Yellow for fog; brown/amber for depth.

q: The protective eyewear standard for racquet sports is:
w: ASTM F803 covers racquet sports eye protection. ANSI Z87.1 is the general industrial standard.

q: Contrast sensitivity in an athlete is measured with the:
w: The Pelli-Robson chart measures contrast sensitivity, which can be reduced even when Snellen acuity reads 20/20.

q: The WHO acuity cutoff defining low vision in the better eye is:
w: WHO sets low vision below 20/70 and blindness at 20/200 or worse in the better eye with best correction.

q: The reference standard for magnification takes 1× as equal to:
w: By convention 4 D equals 1×, based on a 25 cm reference distance. Equivalent add is therefore M × 4.

q: The magnification a low vision patient requires is calculated as:
w: M = required VA ÷ present VA. A patient at 20/200 needing 20/40 requires 200/40 = 5×, i.e. a 20 D equivalent add.

q: The best magnifier for sustained reading in a patient with a hand tremor is a:
w: A stand magnifier rests on the page and fixes the focal distance, so tremor or arthritis does not blur the image. A hand magnifier demands a steady grip.

q: The purpose of a conformer after enucleation is to:
w: The conformer holds the fornices open until the definitive custom prosthesis is fitted; without it the socket contracts and may no longer take a prosthesis.

q: An ocular prosthesis should be professionally polished every:
w: Polishing every 6–12 months removes protein deposits and surface roughness that cause discharge and giant papillary reaction. Adults replace the prosthesis every 5–7 years.

q: The tint of choice in achromatopsia is:
w: Achromats are rod-only and dazzled by ordinary light, so a deep red or orange filter (CPF 550, Wratten #25/#29) cuts the flood and improves function.

q: Besides photokeratitis, chronic UV-B exposure is associated with:
w: UV-B causes acute photokeratitis and, cumulatively, cortical cataract and pterygium, which is the argument for UV 400 protection.

q: Nasolacrimal occlusion after instilling a drop:
w: NLO keeps the drug on the eye and out of the nasal mucosa. It does not weaken the ocular effect: it improves it while cutting systemic load.
k: NLO cuts systemic absorption, not ocular effect.

q: Tropicamide differs from phenylephrine in that tropicamide causes:
w: Tropicamide is an M3 antagonist, so it dilates AND cyclopleges, though weakly. Phenylephrine is the α1 agonist that dilates without touching accommodation.
k: Tropicamide = mydriasis + cycloplegia; phenylephrine = mydriasis only.

q: Diagnostic pharmaceutical agents in pregnancy are:
w: DPAs are Category C, meaning risk is not ruled out rather than proven. When dilation is genuinely needed, tropicamide with NLO is the preferred choice; blanket avoidance is wrong.
k: DPAs Category C: tropicamide preferred when needed.

q: Pseudostrabismus in an infant requires:
w: Pseudostrabismus comes from a wide nasal bridge or epicanthal folds and resolves as the face grows. Hirschberg shows symmetric reflexes, proving no true deviation.

q: Amblyopia treatment after age 7 is:
w: The critical period closes around 7 to 8, and response falls off, but useful gains remain possible to roughly age 12. Beyond that gains are minimal. Never write a child off at 7.
k: Amblyopia: reduced response after 7, some gain to ~12.

q: After prescribing full spectacle correction for amblyopia, you should observe for:
w: Spectacles alone normalize many amblyopes given 12 to 16 weeks of adaptation. Patching is added only if acuity plateaus short of normal.
k: Glasses alone first, 12–16 weeks, then consider patching.

q: Surgery for accommodative esotropia is indicated only when:
w: Full cycloplegic plus is the treatment: the eso is driven by accommodation. Only the non-accommodative residual, still present in the correct glasses, is a surgical target.
k: Operate only on the residual after full plus.

q: Most SEVERE vision loss in age-related macular degeneration is caused by:
o: Wet AMD, despite being the minority
w: Dry AMD is 85% of cases but creeps. Wet AMD is only 15% yet causes most severe loss, because choroidal neovascularization destroys central vision in weeks. Common and dangerous are different questions.
k: Dry = commonest; wet = most severe loss.

q: AV nicking corresponds to which grade of hypertensive retinopathy?
w: Grade 1 is arteriolar narrowing, Grade 2 AV nicking, Grade 3 haemorrhages and exudates, Grade 4 papilledema. Only Grade 4 is the emergency.

q: AREDS2 supplements are correctly described as:
w: AREDS2 slows progression by about 25% in intermediate disease. It treats nothing and reverses nothing, and has no proven role in early AMD.
k: AREDS2 slows progression; it does not treat.

q: Why is a patch the wrong way to test acuity in latent nystagmus?
o: It is uncomfortable for a child
w: Latent nystagmus appears the moment one eye is occluded, so patching produces a falsely poor result. Fogging with a +5.00 lens blurs the eye without triggering the reflex.
k: Latent nystagmus: fog with +5.00, never patch.

q: In cortical visual impairment, the pupils and OCT are:
w: CVI is a brain injury: pupils react normally and OCT shows normal retina. A normal examination in a child who clearly does not see is the diagnostic clue.
k: CVI: normal pupils, normal OCT, abnormal vision.

q: A grey tint affects vision by:
w: Grey is a neutral density filter: it dims without altering colour balance, so contrast is unchanged. Brown and amber lift contrast by selectively cutting short wavelengths.
k: Grey = neutral density, no contrast gain.

q: The ASTM F803 standard applies specifically to:
w: F803 covers racquet sports. Hockey visors and batting helmets fall under their own standards, so quoting F803 for every sport is wrong.

q: Magnification for a low vision patient must be calculated from:
w: Always refract first. Calculating from uncorrected acuity overestimates the magnification needed and lands the patient with a device far stronger and clumsier than necessary.
k: Refract first: magnification from best-corrected VA.

q: The first magnification need of most low vision patients is for:
w: Most patients present wanting to read again, so near magnification comes first. Telescopes address distance tasks and are not a universal starting point.
k: Most low vision patients need near magnification first.

q: A scleral shell is correctly used for:
w: A scleral shell fits over an existing shrunken globe, as in phthisis bulbi or microphthalmos. After enucleation the globe is gone, so a custom prosthesis sits over the orbital implant instead.
k: Shell = shrunken globe present; prosthesis = globe removed.

q: The definitive custom prosthesis is fitted after enucleation at about:
w: A clear conformer holds the socket immediately after surgery, and the custom PMMA prosthesis is fitted at 6 to 8 weeks once swelling settles and healing is complete.
k: Conformer first; custom prosthesis at 6–8 weeks.

q: For mild photophobia or early lens changes, the appropriate filter is:
w: CPF 450, a light yellow, suits mild photophobia. CPF 527 is reserved for RP, macular disease and glaucoma, and darker filters for achromatopsia. Higher CPF number means darker.

q: A 32-year-old pregnant patient needs a dilated fundus exam. Which DPA is most appropriate?
w: All DPAs are FDA Category C in pregnancy. When dilation is clinically necessary, tropicamide is the preferred agent because it has the shortest duration of action and least systemic exposure. Phenylephrine 10% is avoided due to its systemic cardiovascular risks. Cyclopentolate has a longer duration and more systemic exposure. Proparacaine is a local anesthetic, not a mydriatic.
k: All DPAs are FDA Category C in pregnancy.

q: Which of the following best explains why phenylephrine produces mydriasis without cycloplegia?
o: It blocks acetylcholinesterase, preventing ACh from contracting the ciliary muscle
It directly relaxes the ciliary muscle through a non-receptor mechanism
It acts on α1 receptors on the dilator pupillae; the ciliary muscle has no α1 receptors
It acts on M3 receptors selectively on the sphincter pupillae only
w: Phenylephrine is an α1 agonist that stimulates the dilator pupillae. The ciliary muscle is under purely parasympathetic (M3) control — it has no α1 receptors. Therefore, phenylephrine produces mydriasis without any effect on accommodation.
k: Phenylephrine is an α1 agonist that stimulates the dilator pupillae.

q: A 4-year-old with a best corrected VA of 20/80 in the left eye and 20/20 in the right eye is found to have a 3.50D hyperopic difference between the eyes. What is the most likely diagnosis and first treatment step?
o: Strabismic amblyopia; patch the right eye now
Pseudoamblyopia; reassure and retest in 1 year
Deprivation amblyopia; remove the media opacity
w: Anisometropic amblyopia — significant refractive difference (> 1.50D) between eyes, reduced VA in the more ametropic eye, normal alignment. First step is always cycloplegic refraction and full spectacle correction. Patching is added only if VA does not normalize after 12–16 weeks of spectacle wear.
k: Anisometropic amblyopia — significant refractive difference (> 1.50D) between eyes, reduced VA in the more…

q: A parent brings in a 2-year-old whose eyes appear to cross. On Hirschberg testing, both corneal reflexes are centered symmetrically. What is the most likely diagnosis?
o: Strabismic amblyopia — patch the preferred eye
Accommodative esotropia — prescribe full plus correction
Infantile esotropia — refer for surgical alignment
w: Symmetric Hirschberg reflexes rule out true strabismus. The appearance of crossed eyes with normal reflexes is pseudostrabismus, caused by wide epicanthal folds. It resolves as the nasal bridge develops. No treatment is needed.
k: Symmetric Hirschberg reflexes rule out true strabismus.

q: A 68-year-old patient with a 10-year history of diabetes presents with best corrected VA of 20/50 in the right eye. Fundus shows thickening of the retina within 500 µm of the fovea with adjacent hard exudates. What is the diagnosis and appropriate management?
o: Moderate NPDR; observe and recheck in 12 months
Dry AMD with macular drusen; AREDS2 supplements
w: The findings (retinal thickening within 500 µm of fovea + hard exudates with thickening within 500 µm) meet the criteria for clinically significant macular edema (CSME). This requires urgent referral for treatment (anti-VEGF is first-line). This is not PDR (no neovascularization), not AMD (hard exudates in diabetic pattern), and not a watch-and-wait situation.
k: The findings (retinal thickening within 500 µm of fovea + hard exudates with thickening within 500 µm) meet…

q: A 72-year-old patient reports sudden onset of central distortion (wavy lines) in the right eye over 2 days. Fundus exam shows subretinal fluid and hemorrhage near the fovea. What is the most likely diagnosis?
w: Sudden onset of metamorphopsia (wavy lines) + subretinal fluid and hemorrhage at the macula = wet AMD with choroidal neovascularization until proven otherwise. This is urgent — refer for anti-VEGF injection. Dry AMD progresses gradually; DME is associated with diabetes and has a different fundus picture; HTR grade 3 shows flame hemorrhages and cotton wool spots but not subretinal hemorrhage.
k: Sudden onset of metamorphopsia (wavy lines) + subretinal fluid and hemorrhage at the macula = wet AMD with…

q: A 45-year-old diabetic patient reports vertical diplopia that is worst when going down stairs and when reading. On examination, the right eye is hypertropic and the patient tilts the head to the left. Bielschowsky head tilt test worsens the hypertropia when tilted right. What is the diagnosis?
w: Vertical diplopia worst on downgaze + right hypertropia + head tilt to the left (away from affected side) + positive Bielschowsky when tilted toward the hypertropic (right) eye = right superior oblique palsy = right CN4 palsy.
k: Vertical diplopia worst on downgaze + right hypertropia + head tilt to the left (away from affected side) +…

q: A 2-year-old child is referred for poor visual tracking. The parents note that she uses one eye more than the other and has difficulty looking at complex images. MRI shows periventricular leukomalacia. VA testing with preferential looking shows reduced responses. Which diagnosis best fits?
w: History of neurological insult (periventricular leukomalacia — white matter damage) + behavioral visual characteristics (preference, difficulty with complexity, visual latency) + normal-appearing anterior and posterior segments + reduced VEP-based VA = CVI. The diagnosis is behavioral and neuroimaging-based, not based on ocular structural findings.
k: History of neurological insult (periventricular leukomalacia — white matter damage) + behavioral visual…

q: A worker splashes a chemical solvent into their eye. What is the immediate correct action?
o: Apply a bandage contact lens to protect the cornea
Irrigate with clean water or saline for at least 20–30 minutes immediately
Instill proparacaine and refer to the emergency room without irrigation
Identify the chemical first, then call poison control before irrigating
w: Irrigation is the first and most critical action regardless of the chemical involved. Alkali burns (especially) continue to penetrate deeper every second without irrigation. History and identification are done AFTER starting irrigation.
k: Irrigation is the first and most critical action regardless of the chemical involved.

q: A graphic designer working 10 hours daily on a computer complains of eye strain, headache, and blurred vision at the end of the day. Which of the following is NOT part of the management?
o: Prescribing a slightly reduced near add for screen distance if presbyopic
Prescribing cycloplegic drops for daily use to relax the ciliary muscle
w: Cycloplegic drops are diagnostic agents — never prescribed for daily home use. They abolish accommodation, which would make near work impossible and cause photophobia. Management of CVS includes AR coating, the 20-20-20 rule, workstation optimization, and possibly task-specific lenses.
k: Cycloplegic drops are diagnostic agents — never prescribed for daily home use.

q: A tennis player with 20/20 static VA reports difficulty tracking the ball on serve. Which visual skill is most likely deficient and what instrument would you use to assess it?
o: Dynamic visual acuity; Tachistoscope or Marsden Ball
w: Difficulty tracking a moving ball despite normal static VA indicates a dynamic visual acuity problem. The tachistoscope (presents targets for 0.01 seconds) or the Marsden Ball (moving target in three dimensions) is used to assess dynamic VA.
k: Difficulty tracking a moving ball despite normal static VA indicates a dynamic visual acuity problem.

q: A golfer asks what tint would best help them on partly cloudy to sunny days and improve their depth perception for distance judgment. What would you recommend?
w: These tints contain a red element that enhances depth perception — beneficial for judging distance in golf. They work well in partly cloudy to sunny conditions. Gray reduces intensity without improving contrast. Yellow is better for haze and low light. Blue is cosmetically popular but not clinically superior for this purpose.
k: These tints contain a red element that enhances depth perception — beneficial for judging distance in golf.

q: A patient with AMD has best corrected near VA of N32 at 40 cm. Their goal is to read N8 print. What magnification and add power are needed?
w: M = 32/8 = 4×. Equivalent add = 4 × 4 = +16.00D. Working distance = 100/16 = 6.25 cm. The patient must read at approximately 6 cm from the lens.

q: Which of the following best describes the goal of low vision rehabilitation?
o: Train only patients with VA better than 20/200
Maximize function for the patient's daily tasks
Restore best corrected VA to 20/20 with lenses
Replace all spectacles with one magnifier system
w: Low vision rehabilitation aims to maximize the patient's functional use of remaining vision — not restore normal VA. It is task-specific and uses a combination of optical devices, non-optical aids, environmental modifications, and eccentric viewing training based on the patient's needs.
k: Low vision rehabilitation aims to maximize the patient's functional use of remaining vision — not restore…

q: A patient with achromatopsia (complete rod monochromacy) is extremely photophobic and reports glare and difficulty in any lit environment. Which absorptive lens is most appropriate?
o: Green tint for general outdoor use
w: Achromatopsia patients have non-functional cones and rely entirely on rods. Rods are most sensitive in the mid-spectrum (peak ~507 nm) and are overwhelmed by bright light. A deep orange or red longpass filter (CPF 527 or darker) eliminates the short and mid-wavelength stimulation that causes overwhelming photophobia and allows the remaining rod function to operate comfortably. Light yellow (CPF 450) is not sufficient. Gray reduces all wavelengths equally without targeted relief.
k: Achromatopsia patients have non-functional cones and rely entirely on rods.

q: A patient's eye was surgically removed 6 months ago. They are now wearing a stock prosthesis and report daily discharge and socket irritation. What is the next step?
o: Instruct the patient to stop wearing the prosthesis to allow the socket to heal
Refer for a custom PMMA prosthesis fitted by a certified ocularist
w: The stock (ready-made) prosthesis is a poor fit — it cannot conform to the individual socket, which leads to chronic discharge and irritation. The standard of care is a custom PMMA prosthesis fitted by a certified ocularist. Six months is sufficient healing time for proper fitting. Stopping prosthesis wear risks contracted socket, not healing.
k: The stock (ready-made) prosthesis is a poor fit — it cannot conform to the individual socket, which leads to…

q: The UV-B range is:
w: UV-B range: 280–315 nm.

q: The UV-A range is:
w: UV-A range: 315–400 nm.

q: The UV 400 label is:
w: UV 400 label: Blocks all UV below 400 nm.
k: UV 400 label = Blocks all UV below 400 nm

q: The CPF 527 cutoff is:
w: CPF 527 cutoff: < 527 nm (orange).

q: Under the WHO/ICD-11 classification, MODERATE visual impairment is:
w: ICD-11 (2018): mild is worse than 6/12 to 6/18, moderate worse than 6/18 to 6/60 (20/70 to 20/200), severe worse than 6/60 to 3/60, and blindness worse than 3/60 (20/400).

q: Under ICD-11, blindness begins at an acuity worse than:
w: Blindness is worse than 3/60 (20/400) in the better eye with best correction. Severe impairment sits just above it, from worse than 6/60 down to 3/60.

q: In the disorder-to-handicap chain, the psychosocial and economic consequences of vision loss are the:
w: The chain runs disorder (anatomical change), impairment (functional loss), disability (lost skill or ability), handicap (psychosocial and economic fallout such as job loss or isolation). Rehabilitation targets the disability and handicap ends.

q: In the disorder-to-handicap chain, an anatomical change in the visual organ is the:
w: In glaucoma the disorder is the optic neuropathy itself; the impairment is the reduced field, the disability is being unable to read signage, and the handicap is the job loss or isolation that follows.
k: Disorder = the anatomical change (e.g. the neuropathy).

q: How many people worldwide have vision impairment?
w: WHO puts it at 2.2 billion or more, of whom at least 1 billion have impairment that was preventable or remains unaddressed. Prevalence in low- and middle-income regions runs about 4 times higher than in high-income regions.

q: Vision impairment in low- and middle-income regions is more prevalent than in high-income regions by roughly:
w: WHO estimates about a fourfold higher prevalence, which is why 80% of all blindness is considered avoidable: the burden concentrates where cataract surgery and refractive correction are least available.
k: LMIC prevalence ≈ 4× high-income; 80% of blindness avoidable.

q: An acuity of 20/200 corresponds to which M-unit size at 6 metres?
w: The M scale tracks the Snellen denominator: 20/200 = 60 M, 20/100 = 30 M, 20/40 = 12 M, 20/20 = 6 M. M units let you convert a threshold straight into the print size a patient can manage.

q: Kestenbaum's rule estimates the near add as the:
o: Product of distance and near VA
w: Kestenbaum takes the reciprocal of distance VA: for 20/200 the add is 200/20 = +10.00 D. It is a fast starting point, not a prescription.
k: Kestenbaum add = reciprocal of distance VA (20/200 → +10 D).

q: Which method of predicting a reading add is the MOST accurate?
w: Working from measured near VA is most accurate because it uses the near threshold directly and needs no distance VA at all. Kestenbaum and the reciprocal method are both estimates derived from distance acuity.
k: Most accurate add: start from measured near VA.

q: For FLUENT reading (about 160 wpm), the acuity reserve needed is:
w: Spot reading (~80 wpm) needs 1 line of reserve, fluent reading (~160 wpm) needs 3, and maximum reading (~320 wpm) needs 5. Prescribing at threshold gives a patient who can decode letters but cannot actually read.
k: Reserve: spot 1 line, fluent 3 lines, max 5 lines.

q: Enlarging print from 2 M to 8 M is which type of magnification?
w: Relative size magnification enlarges the object itself: 8/2 = 4×. Relative distance magnification instead moves the object closer, and angular magnification uses a lens.
k: RSM = enlarge the object (8 M / 2 M = 4×).

q: Moving a book from 40 cm to 10 cm achieves 4× by which mechanism?
w: RDM, or approach magnification, is the free magnification of getting closer: 40/10 = 4×. It costs accommodation or an add, which is why children do it effortlessly and presbyopes cannot.

q: On the 40 cm reference standard, 4× magnification requires which lens?
w: On the 40 cm (1× = +2.50 D) reference: 2× = +5.00 D, 4× = +10.00 D, 8× = +20.00 D. On the 25 cm reference the same 4× is +16.00 D, so always state which reference you are using.

q: As magnification rises, the field of view:
w: Higher magnification means a shorter focal length and a smaller field of view. This is the central trade-off of low vision prescribing: the strongest aid the patient can hold is rarely the most usable one.
k: More magnification = shorter focal length = smaller field.

q: Which device widens the apparent visual field for a patient with severe field constriction?
w: Field-expansion devices minify: reverse telescopes and minus lenses shrink the image to fit more into the remaining field. Prisms and mirrors relocate peripheral information instead. Magnifiers do the opposite and make constriction worse.
k: Field loss: minify (reverse telescope, minus lens); prisms relocate.

q: The stated goal of low vision device prescribing is summarised as:
w: Bigger (magnification), bolder (contrast), brighter (illumination). Contrast and light are as important as magnification and are far cheaper.
k: Low vision goal: bigger, bolder, brighter.

q: Which lighting type gives high intensity, directional light and a longer life than ordinary bulbs?
w: Halogen is high-intensity and directional with long life. Fluorescent is cheapest but scatters light; incandescent is directional and dimmable but hot; natural light is free but uncontrollable.
k: Halogen = high intensity, directional, long life.

q: Contrast sensitivity loss in GLAUCOMA falls mainly at which spatial frequencies?
w: Glaucoma hits mid spatial frequencies, as does pituitary adenoma. Diabetic retinopathy degrades all grating sizes, while mild refractive error costs only the high frequencies.
k: Glaucoma CS loss = middle spatial frequencies.

q: After refractive surgery, contrast sensitivity typically:
o: Does not recover to the pre-surgery level
w: CS does not fully return to its pre-operative level even when Snellen acuity does, which is why a patient can read 20/20 and still complain about night driving and haze.
k: Post-refractive-surgery CS does not fully recover.

q: Retinitis pigmentosa classically produces which field defect sequence?
w: RP constricts the field: a mid-peripheral ring scotoma closes inward to tunnel vision. Glaucoma's sequence is arcuate scotoma, then nasal step, then tunnel vision, reaching a similar endpoint by a different route.

q: A macular hole produces which visual field defect?
w: A macular hole knocks out the centre, giving a central scotoma. Scattered scotomas suggest diabetic or hypertensive retinopathy; homonymous hemianopsia points behind the chiasm.

q: A whitish pupil (leukocoria) in a child should raise suspicion of:
w: A whitish pupil suggests retinoblastoma or pseudoglioma and is a same-day referral. Greyish-white suggests cataract and brown suggests a nuclear cataract; black is normal.

q: Visual acuity at birth is approximately:
w: A newborn sees about 6/240 (20/800): no coordinated eye movements, responsive to light, vision blurry and shadowy. It reaches roughly 20/400 by one month and adult level near age three.

q: Visual acuity reaches adult level (about 20/20) at approximately:
w: VA is about 20/50 at one year and reaches 6/6 by three, when letter matching (Sheridan Gardiner) becomes possible. By five a child can usually manage a Snellen chart by naming.

q: The pupillary light reaction first appears at:
w: The pupil light reaction is present from about 30 weeks gestation, before birth. Fixation follows at 2 months, smooth pursuit at 6 to 8 weeks, and OKN at 2 to 4 months.

q: Foveal maturation is complete at about:
w: The fovea matures by about 4 months, which is why acuity climbs steeply over the first half-year. Optic nerve myelination continues far longer, from 7 months to 2 years.
k: Foveal maturation ≈ 4 months; myelination to age 2.

q: Which structure is the LAST to develop, at about the 8th month?
w: Roughly: orbit 2nd month, optic nerve and choroid 3rd, cornea and vitreous 4th, lids and lacrimal 5th, iris, ciliary body and retina 6th, lens onset 7th, blood vessels 8th. Late vascular development is why prematurity plus oxygen produces ROP.
k: Blood vessels develop last (8th month) → ROP risk.

q: Retrolental fibroplasia is an older term for:
w: RLF describes the fibrous tissue behind the lens in premature infants given high oxygen, the same entity now called ROP: prematurity plus supplemental oxygen drives abnormal vessel growth, leakage, scarring and detachment.
k: RLF = the old name for ROP.

q: A persistent ATNR (fencing) reflex beyond 6 to 7 months classically disrupts:
w: The asymmetric tonic neck reflex should integrate by 6 to 7 months. Persisting, it ties head turn to arm extension and interferes with reading and writing. A persistent STNR instead causes near vision problems.

q: Which primitive reflex is tested to screen hearing, and suggests ADHD if it persists?
w: The Moro or startle reflex should integrate by 5 to 6 months; it needs an intact auditory pathway, so it doubles as a hearing screen, and persistence is linked to ADHD.
k: Moro = startle, hearing screen, integrates 5–6 months.

q: A Babinski reflex is normal in infants but pathological in adults. It should integrate by:
w: Babinski integrates by about 2 years. Beyond that an upgoing plantar response signals an upper motor neuron lesion, making it one of the few primitive reflexes with hard neurological meaning in adults.
k: Babinski integrates by 2 years; pathological after.

q: The suck reflex is characteristically ABSENT in babies born before:
w: The suck reflex is typically absent below about 32 weeks gestation, so very premature infants cannot feed unaided. The grasp reflex runs the other way and is stronger in premature babies.

q: An ABSENT Landau reflex between 1 and 3 years indicates:
w: Landau is the exception: most primitive reflexes are a concern when they persist, but Landau is a concern when it is absent, signalling a motor development issue.
k: Landau: absence (not persistence) is the red flag.

q: After 20 years of Type 1 diabetes, roughly what proportion have PROLIFERATIVE retinopathy?
w: In Type 1: about 60% show some retinopathy past 10 years, virtually all past 15 years (25% proliferative), and about 50% have PDR past 20 years. Duration is the dominant risk factor.

q: In Type 2 diabetes, what proportion already have retinopathy AT DIAGNOSIS?
w: About 20% of Type 2 patients already have retinopathy when diagnosed, because the disease ran silently for years beforehand. This is why a Type 2 diagnosis warrants a dilated exam immediately, not in a year.
k: Type 2: ~20% have DR at diagnosis → dilate now.

q: After 10 years of Type 1 diabetes, roughly what proportion show some retinopathy?
w: About 60% show some retinopathy past 10 years, rising to virtually all past 15. The progression is duration-driven, which is why screening intervals key off years since diagnosis.
